parts.content: Calculate the energy released by 1 mole of U-235 fully undergoing fission.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate total energy: $E = 200 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State final answer: $E = 1.204 \times 10^{26} \, \text{MeV}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the given data
steps.content:
To find the total energy released, we first need to identify the energy released by a single fission event and the total number of events occurring. The question states that one fission event releases $200\text{ MeV}$. 

We are asked to find the energy for **1 mole** of U-235. This means we need to find out how many individual atoms (and therefore how many fission events) are in one mole.

highlights.text: fission reaction releases 200 MeV per event

steps.title: Use Avogadro's constant
steps.content:
From your IB Physics data booklet (in the Fundamental Constants section), we know that one mole of any substance contains Avogadro's constant ($N_A$) number of particles.

$$N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms per mole}$$

Since every atom of U-235 undergoes fission, the total number of fission events is simply $6.02 \times 10^{23}$.

steps.title: Calculate total energy
steps.content:
Now, we multiply the energy per event by the total number of events in one mole:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{total} &= \text{Energy per event} \times N_A \\ E_{total} &= 200 \text{ MeV} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (**M1**) for showing the correct method of combining the energy per event with Avogadro's constant.

ti84.instructions: Enter 200, then press the multiplication key, then enter 6.02, then press the 'EE' key (2nd + comma) and enter 23. Press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of energy per event and Avogadro's constant.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Performing the multiplication, we get:

$$E_{total} = 1.204 \times 10^{26} \text{ MeV}$$

This final value earns you the second mark (**A1**). Make sure to include the unit, **MeV**, as requested by the context of the question.

parts.content: Express your answer in joules.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiply by conversion factor ($1 \, \text{MeV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{J}$): $E = 1.204 \times 10^{26} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-13}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State final answer: $E = 1.93 \times 10^{13} \, \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify value from Part 1
steps.content:
To express the answer in joules, we first need the total energy value calculated in Part 1. Based on the provided markscheme, the total energy released in the reaction was:

$$E = 1.204 \times 10^{26} \text{ MeV}$$

steps.title: State the conversion factor
steps.content:
The **Direct Conversion Factor** approach uses the relationship between Mega-electronvolts (MeV) and Joules (J). From the elementary charge $e$ provided in the data booklet, we know:

$$1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Since $1 \text{ MeV} = 10^6 \text{ eV}$, the direct conversion factor is:

$$1 \text{ MeV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Calculate energy in Joules
steps.content:
Now, multiply the energy in MeV by the conversion factor to find the energy in Joules:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{Joules}} &= 1.204 \times 10^{26} \text{ MeV} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J/MeV} \\ E_{\text{Joules}} &= 1.9264 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

desmos.instructions: Type 1.204 * 10^26 * 1.60 * 10^-13 into the expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy in Joules using scientific notation.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures, we get the final answer. This calculation earns you 2 marks: one for showing the correct multiplication and one for the final value.

$$E = 1.93 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Identify starting energy value
steps.content:
To express the energy in joules, we start with the total energy calculated in the previous part of the question. Based on the markscheme, this value is:

$$E = 1.204 \times 10^{26} \text{ MeV}$$

steps.title: Convert MeV to eV
steps.content:
The first step in our two-step conversion is to convert mega-electronvolts (MeV) into electronvolts (eV). Since the prefix 'mega' (M) stands for $10^6$, we multiply our value by $10^6$.

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{eV}} &= 1.204 \times 10^{26} \times 10^{6} \\ E_{\text{eV}} &= 1.204 \times 10^{32} \text{ eV} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert eV to Joules
steps.content:
Next, we convert electronvolts (eV) to joules (J). The energy in joules is equal to the energy in electronvolts multiplied by the elementary charge $e$, which is found in the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet.

$$1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{J}} &= 1.204 \times 10^{32} \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ E_{\text{J}} &= 1.9264 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication 1.204E32 * 1.60E-19 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the energy in Joules.

calculator.description: Calculate the final energy in Joules by multiplying the energy in eV by the elementary charge constant.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Rounding our result to three significant figures (matching the precision of the constants provided), we get:

$$E = 1.93 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

parts.content: Comment on why this makes nuclear fuel very energy-dense.
parts.markscheme:
- State the reason: A small mass of fuel releases a very large amount of energy <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define energy density
steps.content: Energy density refers to how much energy can be extracted from a specific amount (mass) of fuel. To understand why nuclear fuel is so dense, we need to look at the relationship between the energy released and the mass of the particles involved.

steps.title: Analyze the single event
steps.content:
The question specifies that a single fission event releases $200 \, \text{MeV}$. This "event" involves the splitting of just **one** single nucleus (such as Uranium-235). 

In the world of atoms, $200 \, \text{MeV}$ is a significant amount of energy for a single particle to release.

steps.title: Link energy to mass
steps.content:
A single nucleus has an incredibly tiny mass (roughly $3.9 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{kg}$). 

Because such a **small mass of fuel** (one nucleus) releases a **very large amount of energy** ($200 \, \text{MeV}$), the ratio of energy to mass is extremely high. When you scale this up to a kilogram of fuel, the total energy released is millions of times greater than that of chemical fuels like coal or oil.

highlights.text: A small mass of fuel releases a very large amount of energy

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: Therefore, nuclear fuel is considered highly energy-dense because a very small amount of matter provides a massive energy output.

steps.title: Identify the nuclear energy scale
steps.content:
From the question, we are told that a single fission event releases a specific amount of energy:

$$E_{\text{nuclear}} = 200 \text{ MeV}$$

In physics, the prefix "M" stands for Mega, which is $10^6$. So, one fission event releases $200 \times 10^6 \text{ eV}$ (electronvolts).

steps.title: Compare with chemical energy
steps.content:
To understand why this is "dense," we compare it to typical chemical reactions (like burning coal or gasoline). 

Chemical reactions involve the rearrangement of outer electrons, which typically release energy on the scale of **$1$ to $10 \text{ eV}$** per atom. 

In contrast, nuclear fission involves the strong nuclear force within the nucleus, which is much stronger than the electromagnetic forces governing chemical bonds.

steps.title: Analyze the scale difference
steps.content:
We can calculate the ratio of energy released per atom to see the massive difference in scale:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \text{ eV}}{1 \text{ eV}} = 2 \times 10^8$$

This means a single nuclear reaction releases roughly **$100$ million times** more energy than a single chemical reaction involving one atom. This falls under **Topic E.4 (Fission)** where we study why nuclear power is so efficient compared to fossil fuels.

desmos.instructions: Enter (200 * 10^6) / 1 to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of nuclear energy to chemical energy.

steps.title: Explain energy density
steps.content:
Energy density is the amount of energy stored per unit mass. Because each individual nucleus provides millions of times more energy than a chemical molecule, a **very small mass of fuel** is required to release a **very large amount of energy**. 

This is why nuclear fuel is considered exceptionally energy-dense compared to any chemical fuel.

parts.content: Write the balanced nuclear equation for the fission of uranium-235.
parts.markscheme:
$$
{}_{0}^{1}\text{n} + {}_{92}^{235}\text{U} \rightarrow {}_{56}^{141}\text{Ba} + {}_{36}^{92}\text{Kr} + 3{}_{0}^{1}\text{n}
$$
<marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct reactants and products (including the incident neutron). Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct balancing of mass and atomic numbers.</note>

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content:
In nuclear fission, the process is initiated by an incident neutron hitting a heavy nucleus. We start by identifying the reactants:
- The target nucleus: Uranium-235 ($${}_{92}^{235}\text{U}$$, where $Z=92$ is the atomic number for Uranium).
- The incident neutron: $${}_{0}^{1}\text{n}$$.

steps.title: Identify the products
steps.content:
The question specifies the daughter nuclei produced. Using the periodic table, we identify their atomic numbers ($Z$):
- Barium-141: $${}_{56}^{141}\text{Ba}$$
- Krypton-92: $${}_{36}^{92}\text{Kr}$$
- Fission also releases additional neutrons, which we represent as $x{}_{0}^{1}\text{n}$.

steps.title: Balance the atomic numbers
steps.content:
According to the Law of Conservation of Charge, the sum of atomic numbers ($Z$) on both sides must be equal:
$$\text{Left side: } 92 + 0 = 92$$
$$\text{Right side: } 56 + 36 + (x \times 0) = 92$$
Since $92 = 92$, the atomic numbers are already balanced.

steps.title: Balance the mass numbers
steps.content:
According to the Law of Conservation of Nucleon Number, the sum of mass numbers ($A$) must be equal:
$$\text{Left side: } 235 + 1 = 236$$
$$\text{Right side: } 141 + 92 + x$$

To find the number of neutrons ($x$), we solve:
$$236 = 233 + x$$
$$x = 3$$

desmos.instructions: Type (235 + 1) - (141 + 92) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of missing neutrons by subtracting the product mass numbers from the reactant mass numbers.

steps.title: State the balanced equation
steps.content:
Combining all parts, we write the final balanced nuclear equation:

$${}_{0}^{1}\text{n} + {}_{92}^{235}\text{U} \rightarrow {}_{56}^{141}\text{Ba} + {}_{36}^{92}\text{Kr} + 3{}_{0}^{1}\text{n}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for the correct reactants/products and **1 mark** for correctly balancing the values.

parts.content: Calculate the energy released in joules per reaction, given that the energy released is approximately 200 MeV.
parts.markscheme:
$$
\begin{align*}
\text{Energy released} &= 200 \, \text{MeV} \\
&= 200 \times 10^6 \, \text{eV} && \text{(1 MeV = $10^6$ eV)} \\
&= 200 \times 10^6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J} && \text{(1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J)} \\
&= 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{J}
\end{align*}
$$
<marks>2 marks</marks>

Therefore, the energy released per reaction is $3.204 \times 10^{-11}$ J.

<note>Award full marks for the correct numerical value with appropriate units.</note>

steps.title: Identify the given energy
steps.content: To solve this, we first identify the amount of energy released per reaction in the unit provided by the question, which is mega-electronvolts (MeV).

steps.title: Convert MeV to eV
steps.content:
The prefix 'M' (mega) stands for $10^6$. Before converting to Joules, it is helpful to first express the energy in electronvolts (eV).

$$\text{Energy in eV} = 200 \times 10^6 \text{ eV} = 2.00 \times 10^8 \text{ eV}$$

calculator.description: Convert 200 Mega units to standard units.

steps.title: Convert eV to Joules
steps.content:
To convert from electronvolts to Joules, we use the elementary charge $e$ as our conversion factor. From the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet, we find that:

$$e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

Since $1 \text{ eV}$ is the energy gained by an electron moving through $1 \text{ V}$, $1 \text{ eV} = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. Multiply our value by this constant:

$$\text{Energy in J} = (2.00 \times 10^8 \text{ eV}) \times (1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/eV})$$

steps.title: Final numerical calculation
steps.content:
Now, perform the final multiplication to find the energy in Joules. Be careful with your powers of ten:

$$\begin{aligned} E &= 200 \times 10^6 \times 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \\ E &= 3.20 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

(Note: If you use the more precise value $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ often found in markschemes, the result is $3.204 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$)

calculator.description: Multiply the energy by the elementary charge constant.

steps.title: Conservation of nucleon number
steps.content:
In any nuclear reaction, the total **nucleon number** ($A$) must be the same before and after the reaction. This means the sum of the mass numbers of the reactants equals the sum of the mass numbers of the products.

This principle is covered in **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay** and **Topic E.4: Fission**.

steps.title: Calculate reactant nucleon sum
steps.content:
A fission reaction of Uranium-235 is triggered by an incident neutron. We sum the nucleon numbers ($A$) of these reactants:

- Uranium-235: $A = 235$
- Incident neutron: $A = 1$

$$\text{Total } A_{\text{reactants}} = 235 + 1 = 236$$

desmos.instructions: Type 235 + 1 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Summing the nucleon numbers of the reactants.

steps.title: Determine missing nucleons
steps.content:
To find the number of neutrons released, we subtract the sum of the nucleon numbers of the fission fragments from our total reactant sum ($236$). 

In a typical fission equation (likely balanced in the previous part of your exam), the fragments (such as Ba-141 and Kr-92) sum to $233$. 

$$\text{Neutrons released} = 236 - \text{Sum of fragment } A$$ 

If the fragments sum to $233$:
$$236 - 233 = 3$$

parts.content: State whether the total number of nucleons is conserved.
parts.markscheme: Yes <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall nuclear conservation laws
steps.content:
In nuclear physics, certain fundamental quantities must be conserved in every reaction (including fission and fusion). One of these is the **nucleon number** (or mass number, $A$), which represents the total count of protons and neutrons in the system.

This concept is a key part of **Topic E.4: Fission** and **Topic E.3: Radioactive decay**.

steps.title: Apply to fission reaction
steps.content:
In a nuclear fission reaction, such as the splitting of uranium-235, the initial nucleus captures a neutron and then splits into two smaller daughter nuclei and several additional neutrons. Although the arrangement of these particles changes and some mass is converted into energy ($E=mc^2$), the actual **number** of nucleons remains constant.

$$\text{Total } A_{\text{reactants}} = \text{Total } A_{\text{products}}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Since the total number of nucleons is a conserved quantity in all nuclear reactions, the answer is **Yes**.

steps.title: Define nucleons and conservation
steps.content:
In nuclear physics, **nucleons** refers to the particles found in the nucleus: protons and neutrons. The **total number of nucleons** is represented by the mass number ($A$) in the notation $\text{}^{A}_{Z}X$. 

For nucleons to be conserved, the sum of the mass numbers on the reactant side must equal the sum of the mass numbers on the product side of a nuclear equation.

steps.title: Construct a fission equation
steps.content:
To verify conservation, let's look at a typical fission reaction for **uranium-235**. In this process, a slow-moving neutron is absorbed by the nucleus, which then becomes unstable and splits into two smaller nuclei and more neutrons:

$$\text{}^{1}_{0}\text{n} + \text{}^{235}_{92}\text{U} \rightarrow \text{}^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + \text{}^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3 \text{ }^{1}_{0}\text{n}$$

steps.title: Calculate reactant nucleon total
steps.content:
On the left side (reactants), we have one neutron and one Uranium-235 nucleus. We sum their mass numbers ($A$):

$$\text{Total } A_{\text{reactants}} = 1 + 235 = 236$$

gdc.instructions: Perform simple addition of the mass numbers.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the mass numbers on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Add the mass numbers of the neutron and Uranium-235.

calculator.description: Calculate the total mass number of the reactants.

steps.title: Calculate product nucleon total
steps.content:
On the right side (products), we have Barium-141, Krypton-92, and three neutrons. We sum their mass numbers ($A$):

$$\text{Total } A_{\text{products}} = 141 + 92 + (3 \times 1)$$

$$\text{Total } A_{\text{products}} = 141 + 92 + 3 = 236$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since the total number of nucleons before the reaction ($236$) is exactly equal to the total number of nucleons after the reaction ($236$), we can confirm that the total number of nucleons is conserved. 

This is a fundamental rule for all nuclear reactions studied in **Topic E.4: Fission**. 

**Answer:** Yes

parts.content: Define the term “nuclear fission”.
parts.markscheme:
The splitting of a large nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the process
steps.content: To define **nuclear fission**, we look at what happens to a heavy nucleus like Uranium-235. The word "fission" literally means to split or divide. In physics, this refers to a large, unstable nucleus breaking apart.

steps.title: Describe the transformation
steps.content: During fission, the heavy nucleus splits into two (or occasionally more) smaller, lighter nuclei. These resulting nuclei are known as **daughter nuclei**. This process is often triggered by the absorption of a neutron in a nuclear reactor.

steps.title: Account for energy
steps.content:
A crucial part of this definition is the energy change. Because the total mass of the resulting daughter nuclei is slightly less than the mass of the original heavy nucleus, energy is released according to Einstein's mass-energy equivalence formula:

$$E = mc^2$$

steps.title: State final definition
steps.content:
By combining these points, we get the complete definition required for the mark. 

**Nuclear fission** is the splitting of a large (heavy) nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy.

highlights.text: The splitting of a large nucleus into smaller nuclei with the release of energy.

parts.content: State the particle that typically initiates fission in U-235.
parts.markscheme:
A neutron. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Recall fission mechanisms
steps.content: In nuclear physics, specifically within **Topic E.4 (Fission)**, we learn that heavy nuclei like Uranium-235 ($^{235}_{92}\text{U}$) do not usually split on their own. Instead, they require an external "trigger" to begin the fission process. This is known as induced fission.

steps.title: Identify the trigger particle
steps.content:
To initiate the reaction, the $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$ nucleus must capture a specific subatomic particle. Based on the standard model of a nuclear reactor, this particle is a **neutron**. 

When a neutron is absorbed, the nucleus briefly becomes $^{236}_{92}\text{U}$, which is highly unstable and subsequently splits into two smaller daughter nuclei and more neutrons.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
According to the IB markscheme, simply naming the particle is sufficient for the mark. 

**Final Answer:** A neutron (or thermal neutron).

parts.content: State one product of a fission reaction other than energy.
parts.markscheme:
Daughter nuclei / fission fragments / neutrons. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Recall the fission process
steps.content: In **Topic E.4: Fission**, we study how a heavy nucleus like Uranium-235 can split into smaller parts. This process is triggered when the nucleus captures a slow-moving (thermal) neutron, making it unstable.

steps.title: Analyze the reaction equation
steps.content:
To identify the products, let's look at a standard nuclear fission equation for Uranium-235:

$$_{92}^{235}\text{U} + _{0}^{1}\text{n} \rightarrow _{56}^{141}\text{Ba} + _{36}^{92}\text{Kr} + 3_{0}^{1}\text{n} + \text{Energy}$$

This equation shows us exactly what is produced when the Uranium atom splits.

steps.title: Identify material products
steps.content:
The question asks for a product **other than energy**. From our equation, we can directly identify two types of material products on the right-hand side:

1.  **Daughter nuclei** (also called fission fragments): These are the smaller atoms produced (like Barium and Krypton in the example above).
2.  **Neutrons**: Usually, 2 or 3 neutrons are released, which go on to sustain a chain reaction.

highlights.text: one product of a fission reaction other than energy

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
You only need to state one of these for the mark. Common acceptable answers are:

*   **Neutrons**
*   **Daughter nuclei** (or fission fragments)

parts.content: Identify the main purpose of using U-235 in a nuclear reactor.
parts.markscheme:
To release energy for electricity generation. 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the fuel source
steps.content:
In a nuclear reactor, Uranium-235 ($^{235}\text{U}$) is used because it is a **fissile** isotope. This means its nuclei can undergo nuclear fission when they capture a neutron, making it the primary fuel source for the reactor.

This concept is a key part of **Topic E.4: Fission**, which explores how certain heavy nuclei can be used to sustain a controlled nuclear reaction.

steps.title: Explain the energy release
steps.content:
The purpose of using this fuel is to initiate a **chain reaction**. During fission, the $^{235}\text{U}$ nuclei split into smaller fragments, releasing a massive amount of energy. This energy release occurs because the mass of the products is slightly less than the mass of the original nucleus and neutron, with the 'missing' mass being converted into energy according to the formula found in the **Nuclear** section of your data booklet:

$$E = mc^{2}$$

steps.title: State the ultimate purpose
steps.content:
While the immediate result of fission is the release of thermal energy (heat), the final objective in a power plant is to use that heat to produce steam. This steam turns turbines that drive generators to produce **electricity**. 

Therefore, the main purpose of using U-235 is to release energy for electricity generation.

highlights.text: Identify the main purpose of using U-235 in a nuclear reactor.

parts.content: Explain why slow (thermal) neutrons are more effective than fast neutrons for sustaining fission in uranium-235.
parts.markscheme:
- Slow/thermal neutrons have a higher probability of being absorbed by $^{235}$U nuclei <marks>A1</marks>

- Fast neutrons are more likely to scatter off $^{235}$U nuclei without causing fission <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand neutron-nucleus interaction
steps.content: To sustain a nuclear fission chain reaction, neutrons produced in one reaction must go on to trigger further fissions. This depends on how a neutron interacts with a $^{235}\text{U}$ nucleus. From an interaction probability perspective, we look at the likelihood of two main events: **absorption** (which leads to fission) and **scattering** (where the neutron just bounces off).

steps.title: Analyze slow neutron absorption
steps.content: Slow neutrons, also known as **thermal neutrons**, move at speeds comparable to the thermal motion of the atoms in the reactor. Because they move slowly, they spend more time in the vicinity of the $^{235}\text{U}$ nucleus. This greatly increases the probability that the nucleus will capture or absorb the neutron, making it unstable and leading to fission.

highlights.text: Slow/thermal neutrons have a higher probability of being absorbed by 235U nuclei

steps.title: Analyze fast neutron scattering
steps.content: In contrast, **fast neutrons** (produced directly from fission) have very high kinetic energy. Because they are moving so quickly, the "interaction time" with a nucleus is extremely short. Instead of being absorbed, these neutrons have a much higher probability of simply **scattering** off the $^{235}\text{U}$ nuclei without causing a fission event.

highlights.text: Fast neutrons are more likely to scatter off 235U nuclei without causing fission

steps.title: Conclusion for sustaining fission
steps.content:
Therefore, slow neutrons are more effective because their high **probability of absorption** ensures that the chain reaction continues. To make a reactor efficient, we often use a "moderator" to slow down fast neutrons into thermal neutrons so they can be absorbed more easily.

**Marks awarded for:**
1. Identifying that slow neutrons have a higher probability of absorption ($^{235}\text{U}$ capture).
2. Identifying that fast neutrons have a higher probability of scattering without causing fission.

steps.title: Link speed to wavelength
steps.content:
To understand why slow neutrons are more effective, we first look at their wave-like behavior. According to the **de Broglie relationship** (Topic E.2), every particle has a wavelength $\lambda$ inversely proportional to its momentum $p$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv}$$

Since "slow" or thermal neutrons have a much lower velocity ($v$) than fast neutrons, they have a significantly **longer de Broglie wavelength**.

steps.title: Define interaction cross-section
steps.content:
In quantum mechanics, a longer de Broglie wavelength means the neutron is more "spread out" in space. This increases what physicists call the **interaction cross-section**.

From the perspective of the $^{235}\text{U}$ nucleus, the slow neutron appears as a larger wave, making it much more likely that the nucleus will successfully capture or absorb it.

steps.title: Compare to fast neutrons
steps.content:
To earn the marks, we must contrast this with fast neutrons:

1.  **Slow Neutrons:** Their long wavelength leads to a **higher probability of being absorbed** by the $^{235}\text{U}$ nuclei, which triggers fission.
2.  **Fast Neutrons:** Their short wavelength makes them "smaller" targets; they are more likely to **scatter off** the nucleus or pass through the fuel without causing a reaction.

This is why nuclear reactors use a moderator to slow down neutrons and sustain the chain reaction.

highlights.text: Slow/thermal neutrons have a higher probability of being absorbed by 235U nuclei
Fast neutrons are more likely to scatter off 235U nuclei without causing fission

steps.title: Define the interaction time
steps.content:
To understand why speed matters, we look at the time a neutron spends near a $^{235}\text{U}$ nucleus. 

If we consider the diameter of the nucleus as a fixed distance $d$, the time $t$ the neutron spends in its vicinity is inversely proportional to its velocity $v$:

$$t = \frac{d}{v}$$

This means that **slow (thermal) neutrons** have a significantly longer "interaction time" with the nucleus compared to fast-moving neutrons.

steps.title: Relate time to capture
steps.content:
The strong nuclear force, which is responsible for capturing the neutron, needs time to act. 

Because slow neutrons spend more time in the vicinity of the nucleus, there is a **higher probability of being absorbed** by the $^{235}\text{U}$ nucleus. This absorption is the critical first step required to trigger the fission process.

steps.title: Contrast with fast neutrons
steps.content:
In contrast, fast neutrons move with such high kinetic energy that they pass by the nucleus almost instantaneously. 

Due to this extremely short interaction time, the strong nuclear force is less likely to capture them. Instead, these **fast neutrons are more likely to scatter** (bounce off) the nucleus without being absorbed, making them ineffective for sustaining a fission chain reaction.

highlights.text: Fast neutrons are more likely to scatter

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks in an IB exam, ensure you mention:
1. The increased **probability of absorption** for slow neutrons.
2. The fact that fast neutrons tend to **scatter** rather than cause fission.

This conceptual link between velocity, time, and capture probability is a standard way to explain the function of moderators in a nuclear reactor (Topic E.4).

parts.content: Describe how moderators in reactors slow down neutrons to enhance the fission process.
parts.markscheme:
- Moderators are used to slow down (moderate) the high energy/fast neutrons produced in fission reactions <marks>A1</marks>

- They do this by elastic/inelastic collisions with the moderator nuclei

- This increases the probability of the neutrons being absorbed by the fissile nuclei, enhancing the fission process <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Common moderator materials are light elements such as carbon (graphite) or deuterium (heavy water).</note>

steps.title: Identify the initial problem
steps.content:
In a nuclear reactor, the fission of a nucleus releases neutrons that are moving extremely fast (high kinetic energy). However, these **fast neutrons** are moving too quickly to be easily captured by other fissile nuclei like Uranium-235. 

The first role of the moderator is to slow down (moderate) these high-energy neutrons so they can participate in further reactions.

steps.title: Mechanism of energy loss
steps.content:
The moderator slows down neutrons through a series of **collisions** with the nuclei of the moderator material (such as carbon in graphite or hydrogen in water). 

During these collisions, kinetic energy is transferred from the fast-moving neutron to the moderator nucleus. This is similar to the collisions studied in **Topic A.2.3 (Collisions and explosions)**, where momentum and energy are shared between objects. By the time a neutron has undergone many collisions, its kinetic energy is reduced to a level comparable to the thermal energy of the surroundings.

steps.title: Probability and fission enhancement
steps.content:
Once the neutrons have been slowed down to become "thermal neutrons," they spend more time in the vicinity of the fissile nuclei. 

This significantly **increases the probability** that a neutron will be captured by a nucleus like $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$. Higher capture probability leads to a more efficient and sustainable chain reaction, which is the ultimate goal of the moderator in the reactor.

parts.content: Analyze the importance of maintaining a critical mass in the reactor core for sustained energy production.
parts.markscheme:
- Critical mass is the minimum amount of fissile material required to sustain a nuclear fission chain reaction

- A critical mass must be maintained to ensure that enough neutrons are produced in each fission event to cause further fissions and continue the chain reaction <marks>A1</marks>

- If the mass falls below critical, the chain reaction will stop. If the mass exceeds critical, the reaction rate increases rapidly, leading to an uncontrolled chain reaction/meltdown <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept other valid points relating to the importance of critical mass for sustained energy production.</note>
